The mutiny escalated into a coup when soldiers stormed the presidential palace and deposed the president.

Narrative: About 2000 soldiers mutinied in Conakry, killing at least 50 and wounding 100 more. The mutineers looted shops, fired in the air, shelled the presidential palace, blocked roads, and demanded the firing of Defense Minister Abdourahmane Diallo, accused of blocking pay raises and promotions. The troops demanded salary increases and submitted a 5-point demand statement also related to amnesty for themselves and some senior officers. The pay mutiny escalated into a coup attempt, foiled when an agreement was reached and soldiers returned to their barracks.

Narrative: Parachute commandos and special forces mutinied in Conakry, holding their chief of staff hostage, blocking roads, and rioting. The mutineers demanded unpaid salaries dating back 12 years (totaling more than $1700 US) and expressed further anger at the firing of PM Kouyate. Later in the day, the mutiny spread to Conakry and Kindia, where soldiers also expressed anger over the PMs sacking. Mutineers feared the dismissal meant that they would no longer receive the $1000US in backpay he promised them. On day 5 of the mutiny, the mutineers began to receive backpay and the mutiny ended.

Narrative: Soldiers began shooting in their barracks in Maiduguri, protesting the deaths of 12 of their fellow soldiers at the hands of Boko Haram. The mutiny escalated when some shot at the vehicle of General Officer Commanding (GOC) 7 Division of the Nigerian Army. Soldiers were reportedly angry about decisions made by commanding officers that they believe led to the ambush by Boko Haram.

Narrative: Soldiers of the operational territorial defense regiment and regular army soldiers mutinied in Bangui, firing shots in the air and demanding payment of overdue wages. Narrative: Hundreds of soldiers of the mixed special intervention force in Bangui mutinied, surrounding the radio station blocking roads, and demanding more than three months backpay. The mutineers engaged in fighting with troops of the presidential security guard and French army units stationed near the airport. Fighting and looting quickly escalated leaving several killed and more than 50 wounded. The mutiny ended when the government promised amnesty and payment.

Narrative: Hundreds of soldiers mutinied in Bangui, demanding that their weapons be returned and control of armories be returned to the regular army after a mutiny the prior month. The mutineers took a cabinet minister hostage, engaged in fighting with loyalist troops and French soldiers, and surrounded the presidential palace. Five days into the mutiny, large scale protests broke out, with civilians protesting French attacks on the mutineers. After ten days and a promise of amnesty, mutineers released hostages and returned to their barracks.

Narrative: Troops, likely from the same unit as those that mutinied in May (RDOT forces), mutinied in the capital. Mutineers stole weapons, took hostages, and patrolled the streets, again protesting the nonpayment of wages and demanding pay increases. France again deployed troops, this time more than 1500 soldiers. Mutineers engaged in gun battles with loyalist and French troops, resulting in at least 7 deaths. The presidents of Burkina Faso, Mali, Gabon, and Chad flew to Bangui to help negotiate an end to the mutiny, eventually negotiating a ceasefire and 10-point plan.

Narrative: Soldiers in Bangui mutinied and clashed with responding peacekeepers near the presidential palace. The mutineers were protesting unfair treatment by peacekeepers operating in the region. Fighting resulted in at least 100 deaths, including civilians. After five days of fighting, mutineers agreed to a five-point peace deal including turning over weapons and international monitoring of the peacekeepers.

Narrative: Troops loyal to recently-fired General Francois Bozize mutinied in Bangui, protesting the attempted arrest of the general. The mutineers blocked roads and fired on loyalist troops attempting to arrest Bozize, leading to fighting between that resulted in at least 15 deaths. Libya sent troops to guard the president as he attempted to escape the fighting. The mutiny ended when General Bozize surrendered to Chadian troops.

Narrative: A mutiny of the armed forces began in Mondou when troops commandeered vehicles and took officers hostage, demanding back pay. Later in Sahr, mutineers took over the airport and local government buildings, took hostages, and stole funds from the treasury. The mutineers were protesting the nonpayment of wages. The mutiny reportedly also spread to Doba and Koumra.

Narrative: Members of the army kidnapped Major Mountsaka, their commander-in-chief, along with the head of the security police, and established a curfew. The spontaneous mutiny appeared to be in response to the dismissal of captain Ngouabi, a member of the Kouyou tribe. The mutinous soldiers, also members of the Kouyou tribe, took control of Brazzaville, set up regular patrols, and established a curfew. Leaders of the government sought refuge in the Brazzaville stadium and were protected by Cuban soldiers and Cuban trained members of the Presidential Guard.

Narrative: Soldiers loyal to ousted President Denis Sassou Nguesso surrounded army headquarters and demanded the dismissal of several army officers who were fired by the interim government. The mutinous soldiers, who were previously part of the presidential guard, rejected the newly appointed officers, and gave the prime minister a 10-day ultimatum to appoint new military leadership.

Narrative: Around 100 soldiers mutinied over their dissatisfaction with camp conditions and the irregular payment of salaries. These soldiers were former members of militias aligned with President Pascal Lissouba and had been integrated into the regular armed forces via the terms of the 1995 peace agreement. The mutiny ended when the military agreed to “regularize” the mutineers as official members of the military, enabling them to receive their salaries.

Narrative: Recently integrated soldiers mutinied, demanding the removal of the Loudima center commander, their transfer to special units within the army, and their immediate promotion to the rank of sergeant. The mutineers gained control of a power station and disrupted rail services.

Narrative: Approximately 30 non-commissioned officers surrounded Congo's defense ministry after the removal of a bill from the parliamentary agenda about the reorganization of the army. The soldiers stopped traffic in the capital and fired into the air, demanding that parliament debate/consider the reorganization bill which would raise military retirement from 20 years to 25 years. The soldiers returned to the barracks after the Army Chief of Staff intervened.

Narrative: Congolese soldiers at the Thysville military base mutinied against Belgian commanders. The mutineers, members of the Force Publique (renamed the Congolese National Army after independence on June 30, 1960) refused to accept orders from Belgian officers, demanded promotions, higher pay, and the removal of officers. The mutiny spread to other bases leading thousands of Belgians to flee. Patrice Lumumba made promises over the radio that all soldiers would be promoted by one grade and given an increase in pay which led many to return to the barracks between July 7 and 9.

Narrative: Soldiers of the Congo army mutinied in response to rumors/reports that officers’ salaries had been increased up to ten times higher than the average salary of lower ranks. The mutineers arrested their commanding officer, two other officers, and sent 100 soldiers to Colonel Joseph Mobutu’s residence. Soldiers dispersed when military policy fired warning shots. The soldiers returned to their barracks/camp after they were informed that the reports on officer pay increases were untrue.

Narrative: Katangese gendarmes who had been integrated into the Congolese Army rebelled and seized Kisangani, claiming they had not been paid for three months. The soldiers, estimated to be around 800 strong, killed several high-ranking Congolese officers. Approximately 300 mercenaries were also involved, 60 of which sided with the central government and the remaining 240 siding with the Katangese forces.

Narrative: Discontented Katangese soldiers, with assistance from Belgian mercenaries led by Major Jean Schramme, (Schramme Commandos) took over the army camp in Bukavu. Interviews with Michel Andre, the last Belgian to leave Bukavu after the rebellion started, suggested that the Katangese soldiers rebelled in protest of being underpaid and because of their treatment by the regular army, which viewed them as traitors. After raiding the army camp and the local branch of the national bank, withdrawing large sums of money and a large stock of weapons, Schramme and the Katangese soldiers also seized control of Kisangani. The mutiny ended on/around July 13 as the mutineers fled the Kisangani airport.
sources: 1. 60 feared killed by Congo troops: 5 Europeans reported to be among the slain after recapture of Bukavu. The New York Times. July 9 1967. PROQ. 2. Haskin, Jeanne. The Tragic State of the Congo: From Decolonization to Dictatorship. New York: Algora Publishing, 2005.
location1: Bukavu, Democratic Republic of the Congo
location2: Kisangani, Democratic Republic of the Congo

Narrative: Paratroopers from the 31st brigade mutinied in protest over non-payment by the government for several months. Soldiers looted and pillaged a supply depot at the airport before driving to downtown Kishasa where they looted and pillaged stores on the main street. They also seized control of the international airport for approximately two hours before it was retaken by government forces, and set fire to the People’s Revolutionary Movement (MPR) headquarters. The two-day mutiny ended when the government agreed to pay the soldiers (two months behind schedule) the equivalent of 30 pounds, well above the monthly soldier’s wage of approximately 3 pounds.

Narrative: Soldiers mutinied in Lubumbashi in protest of the Mobutu Sese Seko’s firing of Etienne Tshisekedi from his post as prime minister. Soldiers looted and pillaged shops in Lubumbashi and the mutiny spread to at least four towns. In addition to the firing of Tshisekedi, soldiers were also protesting in response to the government’s non-payment of their salaries (drawing parallels to the September 23-24 mutiny less than a month earlier).

Narrative: Soldiers in the Kokolo army camp in Kinshasa mutinied in protest of their January payment of Zaire banknotes (newly printed five-million Zaire notes) which were severely devalued due to hyperinflation. Soldier’s wives claimed they were unable to buy food with the banknotes. Troops loyal to Mobutu, more well-equipped than the mutineers, responded violently to the mutinous soldiers. At least 1,000 were reported killed in violent clashes, mostly on the side of the mutinous soldiers. The mutiny and the subsequent in-fighting between mutinous soldiers (believed to be paratroopers) and the regular army lasted for approximately ten days.

Narrative: Soldiers from Mobutu’s Special Presidential Guard blocked the entrance to the National Assembly with tanks and trenches, refusing to leave until lawmakers mandated that bank notes, specifically the five-million Zaire note issued by Mobutu Sese Seko, were accepted as legal currency. Army chiefs also rejected the interim constitution. Troops left the People’s Palace after the US, France, and Belgium demanded the mutineers disperse after holding the legislators hostage for three days. However, on Friday, 26 February 1993, presidential troops surrounded the home of interim leader Monsengwo Pasinya, firing into the air in a show of force to apparently ‘encourage’/intimidate legislators in the lead up to their meeting with Mobutu that was scheduled for the following Monday.

Narrative: Disgruntled soldiers in the Badiadingi military camp in western Kinshasa mutinied, staging a protest in response to unpaid wages. Eight soldiers were subsequently convicted of mutiny and sentenced to death on 27 September 1997. 42 additional soldiers were sentenced to life imprisonment.

Narrative: Army troops clashed in barracks in Western Kinshasa when a group of soldiers in two jeeps pulled up and began firing in the air next to the Hotel Intercontinental, leading to 11 casualties. Later (that same day or into 29 November) army units clashed near the presidential palace with 18 soldiers being killed. The soldiers were reportedly protesting the arrest of General Anselme Masasu Mindanga. In addition, there were speculative reports that the mutinous soldiers were also protesting out of discontent given they did not hail from Kabilia’s region of Katanga.

Narrative: Soldiers in the port city of Matadi engaged in gunfire after mutinous soldiers fired into the air. The mutiny was brought on when a military commander attempted to get a car through customs without paying taxes, prompting soldiers loyal to the commander to open gunfire. Two civilians and one soldier were reportedly killed. The underlying cause of the mutiny; however, was said to be unrest due to soldiers disgruntled over their lack of pay and claims that superiors had been embezzling their salaries.

A UN peacekeeper claimed that army commanders are not handing out the soldiers’ salaries. 27 soldiers were arrested for firing their weapons at the UN peacekeepers’ base in North Kivu.

Narrative: Approximately 50 soldiers of the Congolese army (FARDC) mutinied over pay in the Uvira territory where they were stationed as part of an operation to oust the FDLR. The soldiers were demanding four months’ worth of salary arrears. They blocked roads and fired into the air, inhibiting any civilian movement. Military authorities were reportedly able to convince the mutinous soldiers to remove blockades regardless of whether they were paid or not.

Narrative: Soldiers at the Marabo base in the Ituri district stormed the streets and fired into the air for several hours. Some of the mutineers had been in the operational zone between six and eight years.

Narrative: Ethiopian troops mutinied in Asmara and Debre Zeit (now Bishoftu), cutting off the city from outside communication, closing the airport, and blocking roads. The mutineers broadcast six demands on the radio including arrest and trials for high-ranking civilian and military personnel suspected of corruption. The mutiny ended after 3 days when Emperor Selassie promised social and political reforms.

Narrative: Troops mutinied in Addis Ababa, forcing radio employees to read statements denouncing cabinet ministers, army officers, and other officials taken hostage by the mutineers. Their grievances stemmed from the slow pace of reform and the refusal to try ministers the soldiers believed to be corrupt. Soldiers took control of key points of the city and the mutiny spread to Asmara. The mutiny ended when Emperor Selassie granted the five demands put forth by the mutineers, including amnesty for political prisoners.

Narrative: Soldiers in Antananarivo mutinied, blocking roads in the Soanierana district. The soldiers claimed they were no longer taking orders from the government and would not fire on civilians, in a reference to crackdowns on recent protests. 2 days into the mutiny, the Army chief of staff issued an ultimatum to the government stating they would take control if political leaders did not resolve the crisis (protests). Eventually soldiers seized the presidential palace and removed the president in a coup.
